long_reaction_chains.name: Long Reaction Chains
long_reaction_chains.description: Questions involving multiple reaction steps where each step's product becomes the reactant for the next

structural_patterns: Product of one step becomes reactant of next

counting_enumeration.name: Counting/Enumeration
counting_enumeration.description: Questions asking 'how many' compounds satisfy a given property, or enumeration of specific cases

deep_mechanistic_reasoning.name: Deep Mechanistic Reasoning
deep_mechanistic_reasoning.description: Questions requiring understanding of reaction mechanisms beyond surface-level recall - intermediates, transition states, electron flow

comparative_ranking.name: Comparative Ranking
comparative_ranking.description: Questions requiring comparison of properties like acidity, basicity, electrophilicity, nucleophilicity, stability, bond strength, etc.

questions.question_text:
If the distribution of molecular speeds of a gas is as per the figure shown below, then the ratio of the most probable, the average, and the root mean square speeds, respectively, is
(A) 1:1:1
(B) 1:1:1.224
(C) 1:1.128:1.224
(D) 1:1.128:1
questions.solution_summary: Since the graph is symmetrical about the peak, the average speed and most probable speed coincide. The rms speed is √(3RT/M). So the ratio is up:uav:urms = √(2RT/M):√(2RT/M):√(3RT/M) = 1:1:√(3/2) = 1:1:1.224.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires recall of molecular speed formulas and their ratios.

questions.question_text:
Which of the following liberates O2 upon hydrolysis?
(A) Pb3O4
(B) KO2
(C) Na2O2
(D) Li2O2
questions.solution_summary: KO2 (potassium superoxide) on hydrolysis gives KOH, H2O2 and O2: 2KO2 + 2H2O → 2KOH + H2O2 + O2. Pb3O4 does not react with water. Na2O2 gives NaOH + H2O2. Li2O2 gives LiOH + H2O2.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires knowledge of hydrolysis reactions of oxides.

questions.question_text:
A colorless aqueous solution contains nitrates of two metals, X and Y. When it was added to an aqueous solution of NaCl, a white precipitate was formed. This precipitate was found to be partly soluble in hot water to give a residue P and a solution Q. The residue P was soluble in aqueous NH3 and also in excess sodium thiosulfate. The hot solution Q gave a yellow precipitate with KI. The metals X and Y, respectively are
(A) Ag and Pb
(B) Ag and Cd
(C) Cd and Pb
(D) Cd and Zn
questions.solution_summary: AgCl and PbCl2 both are white precipitates. PbCl2 is soluble in hot water (residue P is AgCl, soluble in NH3 and Na2S2O3). Solution Q contains Pb2+, which gives yellow PbI2 with KI. So X=Ag, Y=Pb.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires knowledge of qualitative analysis and precipitation reactions.

questions.question_text:
Newman projections P, Q, R and S are shown below: [Image]. Which one of the following options represents identical molecules?
(A) P and Q
(B) Q and S
(C) Q and R
(D) R and S
questions.solution_summary: By rotating Newman projections, Q and S represent identical molecules. P and Q are different, Q and R are different, R and S are different.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires understanding of Newman projections and conformational equivalence.

questions.question_text:
For the reaction 2X + Y → P, the rate of reaction is d[P]/dt = k[X]. Two moles of X are mixed with one mole of Y to make 1.0 L of solution. At 50 s, 0.5 mole of Y is left in the reaction mixture. (Use: ln 2 = 0.693). The correct statement(s) about the reaction is(are)
(A) The rate constant, k, of the reaction is 13.86 × 10⁻⁴ s⁻¹
(B) Half-life of X is 50s
(C) At 50 s, -d[X]/dt = 13.86 × 10⁻³ mol L⁻¹ s⁻¹
(D) At 100 s, -d[Y]/dt = 3.46 × 10⁻³ mol L⁻¹ s⁻¹
questions.solution_summary: From stoichiometry, at t=50s, X left = 2 - 2(1-0.5) = 1 mol. Rate = k[X]. Since -d[X]/dt = 2k[X], half-life = ln2/(2k) = 50s ⇒ k = 6.93×10⁻³ s⁻¹ (not 13.86×10⁻⁴). B correct. C: -d[X]/dt = 2k[X] = 13.86×10⁻³. D: at 100s, X left = 0.5 mol, -d[Y]/dt = k[X] = 3.46×10⁻³.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires derivation of rate laws and half-life from stoichiometry.

questions.question_text:
The Fischer projection of D-erythrose is shown below. D-erythrose and its isomers are listed as P, Q, R and S in Column-I. Choose the correct relationship of P, Q, R and S with D-erythrose from Column-II.
[Column-I: P, Q, R, S images; Column-II: 1. Diastereomer, 2. Identical, 3. Enantiomer]
(A) P→2, Q→3, R→2, S→2
(B) P→3, Q→1, R→1, S→2
(C) P→2, Q→1, R→1, S→3
(D) P→2, Q→3, R→3, S→1
questions.solution_summary: D-erythrose is (2R, 3R). P is (2R, 3R) → identical. Q is (2S, 3R) → diastereomer. R is (2R, 3S) → diastereomer. S is (2S, 3S) → enantiomer. So P→2, Q→1, R→1, S→3.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires understanding of Fischer projections and stereochemical relationships.

questions.question_text:
In thermodynamics, the P-V work done is given by w = -∫ dV P_ext. For a system undergoing a particular process, the work done is w = -∫ dV (RT/(V-b) - a/V²). This equation is applicable to a
(A) system that satisfies the van der Waal's equation of state
(B) process that is reversible and isothermal
(C) process that is reversible and adiabatic
(D) process that is irreversible and at constant pressure
questions.sub_topic: Van der Waals equation, Reversible work
questions.solution_summary: The given equation is for reversible work (w = -∫ P dV). For one mole of van der Waals gas, P = RT/(V-b) - a/V². So it applies to any reversible process (isothermal or adiabatic) for a gas obeying van der Waals equation. It is not for irreversible processes.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires understanding of reversible work and van der Waals equation.

questions.question_text:
With respect to the compounds I - V, choose the correct statement(s).
[Images of compounds]
(A) The acidity of compound I is due to delocalization in the conjugate base
(B) The conjugate base of compound IV is aromatic
(C) Compound II becomes more acidic, when it has a -NO2 substituent
(D) The acidity of compounds follows the order I > IV > V > II > III
questions.solution_summary: Compound I is cyclopentadiene (pKa ~16), conjugate base is aromatic (6π electrons) - correct. Compound IV is phenol, conjugate base is aromatic. Compound II (cyclohexanone) becomes more acidic with -NO2 substituent. The acidity order is actually I > IV > III > II > V? Option D is incorrect.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires understanding of acidity trends and aromaticity.

questions.question_text: In the reaction scheme shown below, Q, R and S are the major products. The correct structure of [options with images]
questions.solution_summary: Based on the reaction scheme, the correct structures are options B and D.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires identification of products from multi-step reaction sequence.

questions.question_text:
Choose the correct statement(s) among the following:
(A) [FeCl4]− has tetrahedral geometry
(B) [Co(en)(NH3)2Cl2]+ has 2 geometrical isomers
(C) [FeCl4]− has higher spin-only magnetic moment than [Co(en)(NH3)2Cl2]+
(D) The cobalt ion in [Co(en)(NH3)2Cl2]+ has sp3d6 hybridisation
questions.solution_summary: [FeCl4]−: Fe3+ (d5), weak field → tetrahedral (sp3). [Co(en)(NH3)2Cl2]+: Co3+ (d6), strong field → d2sp3 (octahedral), has 3 geometrical isomers. [FeCl4]− has n=5, μ=√35 BM. [Co(en)(NH3)2Cl2]+ is diamagnetic (n=0). So A and C are correct.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires understanding of VBT, geometry, and magnetic properties.

questions.question_text:
With respect to hypochlorite, chlorate and perchlorate ions, choose correct statement(s).
(A) The hypochlorite ion is strongest conjugate base
(B) The molecular shape of only chlorate ion is influenced by the lone pair of electrons of Cl
(C) The hypochlorite and chlorate ions disproportionate to give rise to identical set of ions
(D) The hypochlorite ion oxidizes the sulfite ion
questions.solution_summary: HClO < HClO3 < HClO4 (acidity), so ClO− is strongest conjugate base (A correct). ClO3− has lone pair on Cl → pyramidal (B correct). ClO− disproportionates to Cl− + ClO3−; ClO3− disproportionates to Cl− + ClO4− (not same set, C wrong). ClO− oxidizes SO3²− to SO4²− (D correct).

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires knowledge of oxoacids of chlorine and their properties.

questions.question_text:
The cubic unit cell structure of a compound containing cation M and anion X is shown below. When compared to the anion, the cation has smaller ionic radius. Choose the correct statement(s).
(A) The empirical formula of the compound is MX
(B) The cation M and anion X have different coordination geometries
(C) The ratio of M-X bond length to the cubic unit cell edge length is 0.866
(D) The ratio of the ionic radii of cation M to anion X is 0.414
questions.solution_summary: The structure is CsCl type (bcc with opposite ions at corners and center). Formula is MX (A correct). Coordination geometries are same (8-coordinate) for both, so B wrong. Bond length = √3 a/2 = 0.866a (C correct). Radius ratio for CsCl is 0.732-0.999, not 0.414 (D wrong).

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires understanding of CsCl crystal structure.

questions.question_text: 5.00 mL of 0.10 M oxalic acid solution taken in a conical flask is titrated against NaOH from a burette using phenolphthalein indicator. The volume of NaOH required for the appearance of permanent faint pink color is tabulated below for five experiments. What is the concentration, in molarity, of the NaOH solution?
questions.solution_summary: Oxalic acid is diprotic (n-factor=2). At equivalence point: meq of acid = meq of base. 5 × 0.1 × 2 = 9 × M (NaOH). M = 1/9 = 0.11 M.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires application of acid-base titration stoichiometry.

questions.question_text: Consider the reaction A ⇌ B at 1000 K. At time t', the temperature of the system was increased to 2000 K and the system was allowed to reach equilibrium. Throughout this experiment the partial pressure of A was maintained at 1 bar. Given below is the plot of the partial pressure of B with time. What is the ratio of standard Gibbs energy of the reaction at 1000 K to that at 2000 K?

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires application of Gibbs energy and equilibrium constant relation.

questions.question_text: Consider a 70% efficient hydrogen-oxygen fuel cell working under standard conditions at 1 bar and 298 K. Its cell reaction is H2(g) + ½O2(g) → H2O(l). The work derived from the cell on the consumption of 1.0×10⁻³ mol of H2(g) is used to compress 1.00 mol of a monoatomic ideal gas in a thermally insulated container. What is the change in the temperature (in K) of the ideal gas? (Given: E° for O2/H2O = 1.23 V, H+/H2 = 0.00 V, F=96500 C/mol, R=8.314 J/mol/K)

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires application of electrochemistry and thermodynamics.

questions.question_text: Aluminium reacts with sulfuric acid to form aluminium sulfate and hydrogen. What is the volume of hydrogen gas in litre (L) produced at 300 K and 1.0 atm pressure, when 5.4 g of aluminium and 50.0 mL of 5.0 M sulfuric acid are combined for the reaction? (Use molar mass of aluminium as 27.0 g mol⁻¹, R = 0.082 atm L mol⁻¹ K⁻¹)
questions.solution_summary: 2Al + 3H2SO4 → Al2(SO4)3 + 3H2. Moles Al = 5.4/27 = 0.2 mol. Moles H2SO4 = 0.05×5 = 0.25 mol. H2SO4 is limiting (0.25 mol requires 0.167 mol Al). Moles H2 = 0.25 mol. V = nRT/P = 0.25×0.082×300/1 = 6.15 L.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires limiting reagent and gas volume calculation.

questions.question_text: ²³⁸₉₂U is known to undergo radioactive decay to form ²⁰⁶₈₂Pb by emitting alpha and beta particles. A rock initially contained 68×10⁻⁶ g of ²³⁸₉₂U. If the number of alpha particles that it would emit during its radioactive decay of ²³⁸₉₂U to ²⁰⁶₈₂Pb in three half-lives is Z×10¹⁸, then what is the value of Z?
questions.solution_summary: ²³⁸U → ²⁰⁶Pb + 8α + 6β⁻. Moles of U initially = 68×10⁻⁶/238 = 0.286×10⁻⁶. After 3 half-lives, fraction decayed = 1 - (1/2)³ = 0.875. Moles of α = 8 × 0.286×10⁻⁶ × 0.875 = 2.0×10⁻⁶? Actually 8×68×10⁻⁶×0.875/238 = 2.0×10⁻⁶. Number = 2.0×10⁻⁶ × 6.023×10²³ = 1.2046×10¹⁸. So Z = 1.20.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires radioactive decay calculations and stoichiometry.

questions.question_text: In the following reaction, compound Q is obtained from compound P via an ionic intermediate. [Reaction scheme image]. What is the degree of unsaturation of Q?
questions.solution_summary: From the reaction scheme, Q has 18 degrees of unsaturation.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires identification of product and degree of unsaturation calculation.

questions.question_text: The 1st, 2nd and the 3rd ionization enthalpies, I1, I2, and I3, of four atoms with atomic numbers n, n+1, n+2, and n+3, where n<10, are tabulated below. What is the value of n?
questions.solution_summary: The values match with Na (Z=11) where I1≈496 kJ/mol. So n+2 = 11 ⇒ n=9.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires recall of ionization enthalpy values.

questions.question_text: Consider the following compounds in the liquid form: O2, HF, H2O, NH3, H2O2, CCl4, CHCl3, C6H6, C6H5Cl. When a charged comb is brought near their flowing stream, how many of them show deflection as per the figure?
questions.solution_summary: Polar molecules are deflected by charged comb. Non-polar: O2, CCl4, C6H6 (3 compounds). Polar: HF, H2O, NH3, H2O2, CHCl3, C6H5Cl (6 compounds). So 6 show deflection.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires identification of polar molecules.

questions.question_text: In the chemical reaction between stoichiometric quantities of KMnO4 and KI in weakly basic solution, what is the number of moles of I2 released for 4 moles of KMnO4 consumed?
questions.sub_topic: Redox stoichiometry, Permanganate in alkaline medium
questions.solution_summary: In slightly alkaline medium: MnO4⁻ + 2H2O + 3e⁻ → MnO2 + 4OH⁻ (×2). 2I⁻ → I2 + 2e⁻ (×3). Overall: 2MnO4⁻ + 6I⁻ + 4H2O → 2MnO2 + 3I2 + 8OH⁻. So 2 moles KMnO4 give 3 moles I2. 4 moles KMnO4 give 6 moles I2.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires balancing redox reaction in alkaline medium.

questions.question_text: An acidified solution of potassium chromate was layered with an equal volume of amyl alcohol. When it was shaken after the addition of 1 mL of 3% H2O2, a blue alcohol layer was obtained. The blue color is due to the formation of a chromium (VI) compound 'X'. What is the number of oxygen atoms bonded to chromium through only single bonds in a molecule of X?
questions.solution_summary: Cr2O7²⁻ + H2O2 + H⁺ → CrO5 (chromium peroxide, blue). Structure: Cr is bonded to 5 oxygen atoms: 4 single bonds (O-O- linkages) and 1 double bond (Cr=O). So number of oxygen atoms attached through single bonds = 4.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires knowledge of chromium peroxide structure.

questions.question_text: The structure of a peptide is given below. If the absolute values of the net charge of the peptide at pH=2, pH=6 and pH=11 are |z1|, |z2| and |z3| respectively, then what is |z1| + |z2| + |z3|?
questions.solution_summary: At pH=2 (highly acidic): all basic groups protonated, acidic groups neutral → net charge +2. At pH=6: zwitterion → net charge 0. At pH=11 (highly basic): acidic groups deprotonated, basic groups neutral → net charge -3? Wait the solution says |Z1|=2, |Z2|=0, |Z3|=3, sum=5.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires understanding of peptide charge at different pH.

questions.question_text: An organic compound (C8H10O2) rotates plane-polarized light. It produces pink color with neutral FeCl3 solution. What is the total number of all the possible isomers for this compound?
questions.solution_summary: Pink color with FeCl3 indicates phenolic -OH. Optically active means chiral centre. The structure must have -CH(OH)CH3 group attached to phenol ring. Isomers: ortho, meta, para positions (3) × 2 enantiomers = 6.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires counting of isomers including stereoisomers.

questions.question_text:
In an experiment, m grams of a compound X (gas/liquid/solid) taken in a container is loaded in a balance as shown in figure I below. In the presence of a magnetic field, the pan with X is either deflected upwards (figure II), or deflected downwards (figure III), depending on the compound X. Identify the correct statements(s).
(A) If X is H2O(l), deflection of the pan is upwards.
(B) If X is K4[Fe(CN)6](s), deflection of the pan is upwards.
(C) If X is O2(g), deflection of the pan is downwards.
(D) If X is C6H6(l), deflection of the pan is downwards.
questions.solution_summary: Diamagnetic substances (all electrons paired) are repelled → upwards deflection. H2O, K4[Fe(CN)6], C6H6 are diamagnetic. O2 is paramagnetic (two unpaired electrons) → downwards deflection. So A, B, C are correct.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires understanding of diamagnetic and paramagnetic behavior.

questions.question_text:
Which of the following plots is (are) correct for the given reaction? ([P]o is the initial concentration of P)
[Options with graphs]
The order of the above reaction is one (SN1) because substrate is a 3° halide.
questions.solution_summary: For a first order reaction: [P]/[P]o = e^(-kt) → ln([P]/[P]o) = -kt (negative slope). t1/2 is independent of initial concentration. [Q]/[P]o = 1 - e^(-kt). The correct plot is A. B is wrong because initial rate ∝ [P]o. C is wrong because [Q]/[P]o vs t is exponential. D is wrong because slope is negative.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires understanding of first order kinetics graphs.

questions.question_text:
Which among the following statement(s) is(are) true for the extraction of aluminium from bauxite?
(A) Hydrated Al2O3 precipitates, when CO2 is bubbled through a solution of sodium aluminate.
(B) Addition of Na3AlF6 lowers the melting point of alumina.
(C) CO2 is evolved at the anode during electrolysis.
(D) The cathode is a steel vessel with a lining of carbon.
questions.sub_topic: Extraction of aluminium, Bayer's process, Hall-Heroult
questions.solution_summary: All statements are correct. (A) 2NaAlO2 + CO2 + 3H2O → 2Al(OH)3 + Na2CO3. (B) Cryolite lowers melting point. (C) Anode reaction: C + O²⁻ → CO + 2e⁻; 2CO + O2 → 2CO2. (D) Steel vessel with carbon lining acts as cathode.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires knowledge of aluminium extraction processes.

questions.question_text:
Choose the correct statement(s) among the following
(A) SnCl2·2H2O is a reducing agent.
(B) SnO2 reacts with KOH to form K2[Sn(OH)6].
(C) A solution of PbCl2 in HCl contains Pb²⁺ and Cl⁻ ions.
(D) The reaction of Pb3O4 with hot dilute nitric acid to give PbO2 is a redox reaction.
questions.sub_topic: Sn and Pb compounds, Redox reactions
questions.solution_summary: Sn²⁺ is a reducing agent (A correct). SnO2 is amphoteric, reacts with KOH to form K2[Sn(OH)6] (B correct). PbCl2 forms [PbCl4]²⁻ complex with HCl, so Pb²⁺ ions are not free (C wrong). Pb3O4 + HNO3 → PbO2 + Pb(NO3)2 is not a redox reaction (D wrong).

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires knowledge of Sn and Pb compounds.

questions.question_text:
Consider the following four compounds I, II, III and IV. [Images]. Choose the correct statement(s).
(A) The order of basicity is II > I > III > IV
(B) The magnitude of pKb difference between I and II is more than that between III and IV.
(C) Resonance effect is more in III than in IV.
(D) Steric effect makes compound IV more basic than III.
questions.solution_summary: III has three -NO2 groups → strongest -M effect → weakest base. IV has -NO2 groups at ortho positions → steric hindrance pushes -NH2 out of plane → no resonance → acts as aliphatic amine → strongest base. Correct order: III < I < II < IV. So A wrong. B: pKb difference I-II vs III-IV? Not sure but C and D are correct.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires understanding of basicity and steric effects in substituted anilines.

questions.question_text: Consider the following transformations of a compound P. [Reaction scheme]. Choose the correct option(s).
questions.solution_summary: Based on the reaction scheme, options B and C are correct.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires identification of products from multi-step reaction sequence.

questions.question_text: A solution of 0.1 M weak base (B) is titrated with 0.1 M of a strong acid (HA). The variation of pH of the solution with the volume of HA added is shown in the figure below. What is the pKb of the base? The neutralization reaction is given by B + HA → BH⁺ + A⁻.
questions.sub_topic: Acid-base titration, pKb from titration curve
questions.solution_summary: Equivalence point is at 6 mL. Half-equivalence point is at 3 mL. At half-equivalence point, pH = pKa = 11? Actually pOH = pKb. From the graph, at 3 mL, pH = 11, so pOH = 3. Hence pKb = 3.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires interpretation of titration curve for weak base-strong acid.

questions.question_text: Liquid A and B form ideal solution for all compositions of A and B at 25°C. Two such solutions with 0.25 and 0.50 mole fractions of A have the total vapour pressures of 0.3 and 0.4 bar, respectively. What is the vapour pressure of pure liquid B in bar?

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires application of Raoult's law and solving equations.

questions.question_text: The figure below is the plot of potential energy versus internuclear distance (d) of H2 molecule in the electronic ground state. What is the value of the net potential energy E0 (as indicated in figure) in kJ mol⁻¹ for d = d0 at which the electron-electron repulsion and the nucleus-nucleus repulsion energies are absent? As reference, the potential energy of H atom is taken as zero when its electron and the nucleus are infinitely far apart. Use Avogadro constant as 6.023×10²³ mol⁻¹.
questions.solution_summary: P.E. of one H atom = -K e²/r = -9×10⁹×(1.6×10⁻¹⁹)²/(0.529×10⁻¹⁰) = -43.5538×10⁻¹⁹ J. For two H atoms (H2), P.E. = -87.106×10⁻¹⁹ J/molecule. Per mole = -87.106×10⁻¹⁹ × 6.023×10²³ J = -5246.49 kJ/mol.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires calculation of potential energy using Coulomb's law.

questions.question_text: Consider the reaction sequence from P to Q shown below. The overall yield of the major product Q from P is 75%. What is the amount in grams of Q obtained from 9.3 mL of P? (Use density of P = 1.00 g mL⁻¹; Molar mass of C=12.0, H=1.0, O=16.0 and N=14.0 g mol⁻¹)
questions.solution_summary: Mass of P = 9.3 × 1.00 = 9.3 g. Molar mass of P = ? From reaction scheme, Q has molar mass 124? The answer is 18.60 g.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires multi-step yield calculation.

questions.question_text:
Tin is obtained from cassiterite by reduction with coke. Use the data given below to determine the minimum temperature (in K) at which the reduction of cassiterite by coke would take place.
At 298 K: ΔH°(SnO2(s)) = -581.0 kJ mol⁻¹, ΔH°(CO2(g)) = -394.0 kJ mol⁻¹, S°(SnO2(s)) = 56.0 J K⁻¹ mol⁻¹, S°(Sn(s)) = 52.0 J K⁻¹ mol⁻¹, S°(C(s)) = 6.0 J K⁻¹ mol⁻¹, S°(CO2(g)) = 210.0 J K⁻¹ mol⁻¹. Assume enthalpies and entropies are temperature independent.
questions.solution_summary: SnO2(s) + C(s) → Sn(s) + CO2(g). ΔH° = -394 + 581 = 187 kJ/mol. ΔS° = 210 + 52 - 6 - 56 = 200 J/mol/K. At equilibrium: T = ΔH°/ΔS° = 187000/200 = 935 K. For spontaneity, T > 935 K.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires application of Gibbs energy and entropy calculations.

questions.question_text: An acidified solution of 0.05 M Zn²⁺ is saturated with 0.1 M H2S. What is the minimum molar concentration (M) of H⁺ required to prevent the precipitation of ZnS? Use Ksp(ZnS) = 1.25×10⁻²² and overall dissociation constant of H2S Knet = K1K2 = 1×10⁻²¹.
questions.sub_topic: Solubility product, Common ion effect, Precipitation
questions.solution_summary: For precipitation to be prevented: [Zn²⁺][S²⁻] < Ksp. [S²⁻] = Knet × [H2S]/[H⁺]² = 1×10⁻²¹ × 0.1/[H⁺]² = 1×10⁻²²/[H⁺]². At precipitation threshold: 0.05 × 1×10⁻²²/[H⁺]² = 1.25×10⁻²² ⇒ [H⁺]² = 0.05×1×10⁻²²/1.25×10⁻²² = 0.04 ⇒ [H⁺] = 0.20 M.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires understanding of solubility product and common ion effect.
